data.title: Conestoga Capital Advisors Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Equity markets began the new year with a rally on enthusiasm for an economy that would remain strong and a forecast for moderating inflation, lower interest rates, an extension of lower tax rates, and lower regulation. But rising concerns about a slowdown in earnings from large capitalization Technology companies, geopolitical tensions, and the President's announcement that tariffs would be detailed in early April pushed equities sharply lower to end the first quarter. Investors shifted to safety and U.S. Treasury yields fell.Small capitalization stocks as measured by the Russell 2000 declined nearly 11% in the first quarter and were down roughly 20% from their post-election highs in November 2024. We expect our investment approach to outperform in periods of heightened uncertainty, and we can report that our Micro, SMid and Mid Cap Growth strategies did indeed outperform in the first quarter. Our Small Cap Growth strategy held several stocks that experienced company-specific issues which led to modest underperformance relative to its benchmark in the first quarter. Performance for each of our primary strategies may be found in the below table, and detailed attribution commentary is available on the pages that follow.

Firm Update

In March, Conestoga announced succession plans for its flagship Small Cap Growth strategy. At the end of 2025, Derek Johnston, Partner/Portfolio Manager, will succeed Joe Monahan as Co-Portfolio Manager for Conestoga's Small Cap Growth strategy. Joe Monahan will continue to serve as Co-Portfolio Manager for Conestoga's Micro Cap Growth strategy and as an analyst supporting the firm's strategies until his retirement in late 2026.

Derek joined Conestoga in 2015, was named Co-Portfolio Manager for the SMid Cap Growth strategy in 2016 and became a partner in 2018. Derek has played a key role in growing the firm's SMid Cap Growth total assets from less than $100 million to nearly $2 billion. Along with his promotion to Co-Portfolio Manager for the Small Cap Growth strategy, Derek is also being elevated to a Managing Partner of Conestoga Capital Advisors, LLC. Derek's investment experience spans 29 years, which includes his experience prior to joining Conestoga at 300 North Capital, Engemann Asset Management and Banc of America Securities. He has a Bachelor of Science degree from Boston College and a Master of Business Administration degree from the Marshall School of Business at the University of Southern California. Derek earned the right to use the Chartered Financial Analyst ® designation in 2003.

Joe Monahan joined Conestoga in 2008 and has served as Co-Portfolio Manager of the Small Cap Growth strategy and Director of Research since the beginning of 2014. Joe's contributions to the firm have been significant and he played a key role in growing the firm's assets under management from less than $1 billion to nearly $7.8 billion today.

David Neiderer, Partner/Portfolio Manager, will assume Joe's role as a Director of Research and will also be elevated to a Managing Partner of Conestoga Capital Advisors, LLC. David joined Conestoga in 2013 and became a partner in 2018. David will also continue to serve as Co-Portfolio Manager for the firm's Micro Cap Growth strategy. David earned his Bachelor of Science degree in Economics and Accounting from the University of Richmond and a Master of Business Administration from The Wharton School at the University of Pennsylvania. He is a CFA Charterholder® and also a Certified Public Accountant.

As of March 31, 2025, Conestoga's total assets were $7.8 billion. Assets within our four primary institutional investment strategies were:

Our Initial Thoughts on the Tariffs Announcements

What are the potential ramifications of higher tariff rates on the Conestoga portfolios? It is quite difficult at this juncture to handicap the various potential outcomes given the fluid environment. With the Russell 2000 Growth down over 25% from its December high, a recession may already be embedded in market expectations.

We believe our companies are well positioned to handle this challenge. In Trump's first administration, we also faced tariff issues (albeit not as dramatic), but our companies repositioned their supply chains to adjust to the new environment. Additionally, our companies have historically had strong pricing power and highly differentiated products or services which enables them to better pass along higher costs generated by the tariffs. In addition, our companies' strong balance sheets and cash flow position them well to take advantage of the economic uncertainty.

In fact, the Conestoga strategies outperformed by meaningful margins from March 24th (when President Trump announced he would unveil new tariffs on automotive imports) through the completion of this letter on April 15th . The market's decline during this period was exacerbated by the bolder-than-expected reciprocal tariff announcement on April 2nd. From March 24th to April 15th, Conestoga's Small, SMid, Mid and Micro Cap Growth strategies all outperformed their benchmarks by several hundred basis points. Industries in which we typically do not invest were under particular pressure including semiconductors, retail, apparel, and commodity-based stocks. Furthermore, our Technology, Industrials, and Basic Materials sector holdings performed very well versus the benchmark constituents.

Over the past several months, we have focused our research efforts identifying the companies in the portfolios that we believe have a higher exposure to the tariffs given their supply chain and/or manufacturing footprint. Furthermore, we believe the domestic orientation of our portfolio positions us well. For example, the Small Cap Growth portfolio's revenue is predominantly sourced from U.S. ( ∼75% ) versus international ( ∼25% ) markets. While the tariffs are adversely impacting the equity markets and uncertainty may weigh on companies' near-term earnings, we also believe our small cap companies will benefit from the reshoring that is expected to take place because of the tariffs. Additionally, the Administration's focus on lower tax rates and less regulation should favor small cap companies. Our companies are well managed and focus on their long-term positioning and earnings power. We believe over the long-term our portfolio holdings will successfully manage this challenge through a combination of pricing power and more efficient operations. We will continue to monitor market developments and assess the impact of the tariffs on our portfolio companies.

Conestoga's Investment Philosophy and Approach

Philosophy

Our high quality conservative growth philosophy seeks to take advantage of the inefficient discovery process for micro, small, smid and mid capitalization companies and other investors' focus on near-term earnings. We employ our 'time horizon arbitrage' principles by identifying these higher quality companies that we believe are capable of growing through multiple business cycles.

Key Tenets of Our Style

  • High Quality Conservative Growth
    • We seek to invest in companies which we believe have sustainable earnings growth and strong balance sheets.
  • Patient, Long-Term Approach
    • We have a long-term investment horizon which typically results in a low turnover rate of 20-30%.
  • High Conviction
    • Range of portfolio holdings is expected to provide a balance between alpha generation and diversification.
  • Consistency of Returns with Low Volatility and Downside Capture
    • Consistently applied investment discipline has resulted in strong risk-adjusted returns over full market cycles.

data.title: Dividend Power Dogs: 3 Ideal Safe Buys For May
data.description:
Foreword

Only one on this Dividend Power list is too pricey, or reveals skinny dividends! Three of these thirty-four low-priced Dividend Power dogs are ready to buy because they also show “safer” dividends whose free cash-flow yield exceeds dividend yield. May finds: ZIM Integrated Shipping (ZIM), Mesabi Trust (MSB); Golden Ocean (GOGL), as the top three, living up to the dogcatcher ideal.

About Dividend Power

“How high are the earnings?

This strategy defines a universe of stocks based on earnings yield, with higher earnings yield considered better, because it signifies a low valuation.

How great is the yield?

We sort the universe of stocks by their dividend yield, picking the top 35.

What is the result?

The result is a portfolio that does a relatively good job weathering downturns, while still catching much upward motion during bull markets.

It is one of the simplest strategies that has also proven to be one of the most effective.”—YCharts

[See a summary of top ten break-even priced May Dividend Power Dogs (below actionable conclusion #21 mid-article) and four “safer” choices in the Afterword, at the bottom of this article.]

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 39.74% To 110.71% Net Gains From Top-Ten DiviPower Dogs By May 2026

Five of ten top DiviPower stocks (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this May 2025 yield-based forecast, as graded by Wall St. wizard estimates, was 50% accurate.

Estimated dividend-returns from $1000 invested in the ten highest-yielding stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, created the 2025-26 data points for the projections below. Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to May 2026 were:

Cool Co Ltd (CLCO) was projected to net $1,107.12 based on the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. A Beta number was not available for CLCO.

Seanergy Maritime Holdings (SHIP) was projected to net $953.99 based on the median of target price estimates from 4 analysts, plus the estimated annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 21% more than the market as a whole.

Mach Natural Resources (MNR) was projected to net $843.45, based on dividends, a target price estimate from 4 analysts, less broker fees. A beta number was not available for MNR.

Advanced Flower Capital (AFCG) was projected to net $830.55 based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 5 analysts, less broker fees. A Beta number was not viable for AFCG.

MFA Financial Inc (MFA) was projected to net $629.58 based on the median of target prices estimated by 6 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 69% over the market as a whole.

ZIM Integrated Shipping was projected to net $572.70, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 7 analysts, less broker fees. A Beta number was not viable for ZIM.

Okeanis Eco Tankers (ECO) was projected to net $479.97 based on the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 99% less than the market as a whole.

SFL Corp (SFL) was projected to net $467.06, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 4 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 53% less than the market as a whole.

Civitas Resources (CIVI) was projected to net $436.54, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 14 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 9% over the market as a whole.

SITE Centers (SITC) was projected to net $397.41, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 4 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 46% over the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 67.18% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten DiviPower stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 38% under the market.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs".

Top 35 May 2025 DiviPower Dogs By Yield

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): 10 Top May DiviPower Stocks By Yield Ranged 13.77% To 74.11% Per YCharts

Top ten DiviPower stocks occupied five of eleven Morningstar sectors.

First place went to the lone industrials stock ZIM Integrated Shipping [1]. then. two energy representatives placed second and tenth, Mach Natural Resources [2], and BW LPG Ltd (BWLP) [9].

The first of five financials stalwarts, placed third, Triple Point Venture Growth BDC (TPVG) (3. The other four placed fifth to seventh and ninth, Advanced Flower Capital [5], OFS Capital (OFS) [6], MFA Capital [7], and Trinity Capital (TRIN) [10].

In fourth place stood the lone real estate member, SITE Centers [5],

Finally, in eighth place, the lone utilities member, Kenon Holdings Ltd (KEN) stood firm, to complete the May DiviPower list, by yield.

Top 35 May 2025 DiviPower Dogs By Analyst Target Prices

Dividend Vs. Price Results

The top graph above shows relative strengths of the top ten May DiviPower list by dividend and price as of market close 5/13/2026.

This month 34 of the 35 DiviPower list, by yield, show projected annual dividends from $1k invested greater than their single share price.

Dividend Power rules! The lowest Power Dog cost per dollar of dividend was $1.35 from ZIM and the highest cost dividend dollar was exhibited by Bain Capital at $129.00 and Bain also failed to meet the dogcatcher Ideal.

A dividend dogcatcher priority is to select stocks whose dividends from $1K invested are greater than their single share price. As mentioned above, that condition was reached by 34 of 35 on the May dividend power list.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Twenty of Thirty-Five DiviPower Dogs Show Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividends. Eighteen Dividend Power stocks so infected are MNR, SITC, MFA, KEN, TRIN, AOMR, REFI, SFL, SCM, PFLT, SEVN, EFC, MFIC, NCDL, OBDC, CLCO GAIN, SHIP, RYN, and FPI. These are considered unsafe to buy.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pullbacks in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, DOGE, Tariffs, Deals, and Uncertainty, too!

Ten Top Yield Dividend Power Dogs Show "Fair Price" Rates For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, all in the top ten are already ideally priced and their prices are all more than 65% below break-even.

Mr. Market is very effective at moving prices up or down to appropriate amounts. Watch and buy when your targeted stock price moves to a sweet spot.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 24.36% To 102.7% Top Ten DiviPower Upsides To May 2026; (32). Four -0.36% To -86.02% Downsiders Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside-potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 8.21% Disadvantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 DiviPower Dogs As of April 4, 2026

Ten-top DiviPower dogs were culled by yield for this monthly update. All ten showed positive yield (dividend / price) results. YCharts did the ranking.

As noted-above, top-ten DiviPower dogs selected 5/13/25 by yield, represented five of eleven sectors: Industrial, Financial, Energy, Utilities, and Real Estate. Missing the top-ten cut were Consumer Discretionary, Consumer Staples, Communication Services, Health Care, Materials, and Technology.

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of Ten Highest-Yield Dividend Power Dogs (34) To Deliver 42.15% Vs. (35) 36.64% May 13, 2026

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten Dividend Power list by-yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 15.03% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The seventh lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, Mach Natural Resources showed top analyst-estimated gains of 84.35%.

The five lowest-priced top-yield Dividend Power dogs for May were: Advanced Flower Capital; TriplePoint Venture; OFS Capital; MFA Financial; BW LPG, with prices ranging from $5.77 to $11.46.

Five higher-priced Dividend Power dogs as of May 13 were: SITE Centers; Mach Natural Resources;Trinity Capital; ZIM Integrated Shipping; Kenon Holdings, with prices ranging from $12.42 to $32.66

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As the post-election euphoria fades and the feckless Fed-fueled slide and dithering presidential pronouncements gain momentum into 2025 with the exception of the few AI stocks, watch all of the top ten Power dogs stay Fair-priced…

Afterword

Buy 3 Ideal ‘Safer' May Dogs of Dividend Power

April, 2025, 4 of the 35 Dividend Power Dogs were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns and free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields. YCharts 5/13 data showed four as fair-priced,

Dividend Power 'Safer' Dividend Vs. Price “Break-Even” Results For Investors

The chart at the top above reveals that all dividends from $1K invested in our top three ’safer' Dividend Power dogs conform to the dogcatcher standard of being equal to (or greater than) the price of a single share.

The middle chart shows the recent dividend amount and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) equal to the single share price of each stock; a so-called, “break-even price.”

No market correction is required to put all three share prices of these “safer” survivors above or equal to their dividend payouts from $1k invested.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher Dividend Power updates, out here, in public, away from the pay-walled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

Three IDEAL ‘Safer” Dividend Power Dogs

The net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" Dividend Power dog dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations.

Nothing contained herein shall constitute a solicitation, recommendation or endorsement to buy or sell any security. Prices and returns on equities in this article except as noted are listed without consideration of fees, commissions, taxes, penalties, or interest payable due to purchasing, holding, or selling same.

Graphs and charts were compiled by Rydlun & Co., LLC from data derived from YCharts; Yahoo Finance - Stock Market Live, Quotes, Business & Finance News; analyst mean target price by YCharts. Open-source dog art from dividenddogcatcher.com.

data.title: INDY: Look Through India's Border Tensions
data.description:
Geopolitical risks have risen in India following the launch of “Operation Sindoor”, a military response to last month’s terror attack in Pahalgam (considered the “deadliest attack on civilians in India” since 2008), which resulted in twenty-six civilian casualties. Pakistan has since retaliated against the Indian strikes, making this one of the more severe India-Pakistan conflicts in recent memory.

Despite the rapid escalation, this one has been very much unlike past crises in that both sides have also rapidly taken an off-ramp in the form of a (rather fragile) cross-border ceasefire.

This is largely thanks to where the geopolitical incentives lie this time around. On the one hand, Pakistan has the backing of China, but not the West, which appears more in favor of neutrality this time around. Add to that the push by the Trump administration, using trade (note India and the US are currently hashing out a trade deal) as a de-escalation tool, and you have all the ingredients for a timely off-ramp.

In any case, Indian markets have, rightly in my view, seen through the latest India-Pakistan conflict after a momentary dip. Prior years’ precedents (2019 Pulwama attack, 2016 Uri attack) suggest staying long equities is the way to go as well.

In sum, I would stay focused on India’s fundamentals, which screen attractively now that earnings numbers are tracking ahead of consensus this quarter. With foreign investor flows also rapidly reversing after a prolonged selloff (see INDY: Weathering The Storm With Indian Mega Caps), iShares India 50 ETF (INDY) stands out as a prime beneficiary.

INDY Overview – Focused on India's Mega Caps

Within the Indian ETF universe, INDY stands out from key comparables like the iShares MSCI India ETF (INDA) and Franklin Templeton's ultra-low-cost FTSE India ETF (FLIN) by concentrating on India’s fifty largest blue chips, as tracked by the flagship Nifty 50 index. There is one other US-listed Nifty 50 tracker, First Trust’s India Nifty 50 Equal Weight ETF (NFTY), though, unlike INDY’s capitalization weighting, NFTY is equal-weighted (see NFTY: India Enters The 'Panic Zone').

INDY isn’t quite as popular, though, with its $694m asset base paling in comparison to INDA’s $9.1bn and FLIN’s $2.2bn. Less size means less liquidity, as evidenced by its wider 0.12% bid/ask spread (vs. 0.02% for INDA and 0.08% for FLIN). Combined with this is a premium ~0.9% expense ratio (vs. ~0.6% for INDA and ~0.2% for FLIN), which makes this ETF not quite as fundamentally competitive as comparable Indian large-cap vehicles.

INDY Portfolio – Mind the Concentration

Post-rebalancing, INDY’s 50-stock portfolio is now more concentrated than before at the sector level. A lot of this is down to the resilience of its biggest sector exposure, Financials - an even larger overweight at 37.2%. By comparison, INDA runs its Financials allocation at almost eight points lower at 29.3%, while FLIN’s exposure is an even lower 28.6%. Other major sector allocations here include Information Technology (down to 11.5% amid tariff turbulence), Consumer Discretionary (up to 11.0%), and Energy (flat at 10.2%).

INDY’s single-stock allocation is also quite concentrated. In line with last quarter, the fund’s largest holdings remain India’s two major private banking groups, HDFC Bank (HDB) and ICICI Bank (IBN), albeit at an upsized 13.1% and 9.0%, respectively. Conglomerate Reliance Industries (RLNIY) is also up to 8.6% after a strong year, though IT services leader Infosys (INFY) has lost ground. In total, the top five contribute an outsized 40.4% (vs 28.4% for INDA and 25.7% for FLIN), so investors will want to be comfortable with a high level of concentration here.

Expect some changes, however, ahead of a major MSCI index “rejig” later this month. On the one hand, India will not only maintain its MSCI Emerging Markets weighting (second-largest at ~19.5%) but also net gain additional passive inflows from the move. On the other hand, INDY will see many of its larger holdings, from HDFC Bank to Infosys, downsized by MSCI in favor of smaller holdings like Cipla, Ultratech Cement, and Grasim. Net-net, I do expect concentration levels to ease as a result, though the impact shouldn’t be too material.

INDY Performance – Leading Through the Bear Market

India’s bull markets tend to be led by higher-growth smaller caps, but in bear markets, like the one we saw in recent months, mega-cap-focused INDY outperforms. Year-to-date, INDY is the pick of the large-cap trackers at +3.9% (vs +1.9% for INDA and +1.9% for FLIN); similarly, over the last one year, INDY’s total return stacks up nicely against both INDA and FLIN.

Zoom out, however, and the fund has lagged its ETF comparables, albeit with very respectable +14.7% and +7.5% annualized NAV returns over the last five and ten years, respectively. The key is that, because in the last decade, India has been in a raging bull market, the mega caps have relatively underperformed smaller caps. In a bear market, on the other hand, the inverse tends to hold true. In essence, owning INDY is less about maximizing upside than balancing this upside with some downside protection as well.

The other plus to owning INDY is that it also tends to trade at a lower forward P/E than other India trackers. And having de-rated quite a bit in recent months, largely thanks to heavy foreign investor selling, this Nifty 50 tracker now looks quite undemanding relative to its outlook for mid-teens % earnings growth and returns on equity.

Summing Up

Ongoing India-Pakistan border tensions represent a tail risk, but if history is any indication, staying long in Indian equities tends to pay off eventually. In the meantime, there’s a growing technical case to be long INDY, given mega-caps are prime beneficiaries of a resurgence in foreign investor flows. A fundamental one too, given India’s relatively low exposure to trade-related disruptions, and with earnings tracking ahead of expectations this reporting season. Net-net, INDY offers a compelling risk/reward here.

data.title: AMD: A Losing Bet On AI
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) recently reported its Q1 results. While the results were decent, as its revenues increased by 36% Y/Y, we see several risks that the company faces, which in our opinion are likely to undermine AMD’s growth opportunities. Although AMD’s stock price has slightly increased after our bearish call in March, we still believe that the company is uninvestable.

Why We Remain Bearish About AMD

One of the biggest issues that AMD currently faces is the implementation of stricter export controls by the United States, which are going to affect the company’s performance in Q2 and the rest of 2025. AMD’s Q2 guidance already reflects a $700 million revenue reduction as a result of new U.S. export restrictions on Instinct MI308 GPUs shipped to China. Overall, the management currently estimates a negative impact of $1.5 billion in 2025, which is a major issue in our opinion.

While AMD will likely try to offset the China-related losses by trying to grow sales in the United States and other markets, there’s a risk that such a plan might fail considering the recent weakening of the American economy. Also, China has been a significant early adopter of AMD’s AI accelerators. The loss of the Chinese market makes it harder for AMD to aggressively grow at a time when Nvidia’s (NVDA) dominance in the AI GPUs business remains unchallenged.

Another way to offset the China-related losses would be to launch the next generation of AI accelerators. That’s exactly what the company is planning by accelerating the launch of GPUs from the MI350 series this year. However, there’s no guarantee that an accelerated launch is going to greatly help the company in the end. So far, no major AI lab or a big large language model ("LLM") player has publicly committed to large-scale deployment of infrastructure based on the MI350 chips. This is why we believe that it’s too optimistic to believe that the GPUs from the MI350 series will save the day.

Another risk that AMD faces is the fading of the momentum in the client and gaming segments. Last quarter, AMD’s gaming revenue fell by 30% Y/Y due to weak demand for semi-custom products. Although the sales of Radeon graphics cards were recently strong, they are unlikely to offset the decline in console-related SoC sales later this year, especially given the maturing console cycles.

All of this makes us remain bearish about AMD and its future.

Risk To Our Bearish Thesis

Despite our bearish stance, it’s important to highlight AMD’s strengths to figure out if they would be able to undermine our thesis.

One major thing that AMD has going for it is the growth of the data center processor market, which is not stopping for now and could help AMD boost its overall sales in the future despite all the risks. While Nvidia dominates the market for now, AMD is currently establishing itself as a solid second option for AI accelerators.

Also, AMD has a diversified portfolio of products, which is another of its strengths. With its exposure to desktop, mobile, and could markets, AMD is diversified enough to weather the current macro volatility and potentially perform better than expected in the upcoming months.

The Real Value of AMD

To determine AMD’s intrinsic value, we created a valuation model. The valuation model was created when AMD was trading at $102.84 per share. We applied a perpetual growth rate of 3% in our model, consistent with the rate we typically use when valuing other semiconductor businesses. The forecast covers the upcoming five years, with inputs such as cash and long-term debt sourced from the latest earnings report.

The discount rate in the model is 10.46%. We calculated it by figuring out AMD’s cost of equity and cost of debt. To calculate the cost of debt, we mostly used its TTM numbers. For the cost of equity calculation, we used a risk-free rate of 4.38%, a beta of 1.87, and a market return rate of 7.77%. Later, we weighted AMD’s debt and equity to figure out its discount rate.

In the forecast table, we largely align our sales and EBIT projections with the broader market consensus for the next five years. We also assume a 15% tax rate in the model, consistent with the rate applied in our earlier valuations of semiconductor companies. The changes in net working capital in our model are expected to be 1% of sales, which is the rate that we also used when creating a valuation model for other semiconductor businesses. The rest of the assumptions in the model closely align with AMD’s historical performance.

Under those assumptions, our valuation model estimates AMD’s enterprise value to be $87 billion. By adding cash and cash equivalents to the enterprise value and subtracting the total debt, we can calculate AMD’s equity value, which in our case is $88.4 billion. By dividing the equity value by the number of outstanding shares, we arrive at AMD’s intrinsic value of $54.00 per share, which is about 47.49% below the current market price.

Final Thoughts

The loss of China-related revenues as a result of the stricter export restrictions and the overvaluation of the company’s shares makes us believe that AMD remains uninvestable at this point. It certainly has some strengths, but they are not enough to justify a long position in the company. That is why AMD remains a Sell for us.

data.title: Buckle Up For An Economic Deceleration
data.description:
Stocks defied gravity for a fourth session in a row, thanks to lower Treasury yields across the curve on the tailcoats of mixed economic data that gave investors hope for more interest rate cuts from the Fed. The reasoning changes from one day to the next, but this runaway train that pulled out of the station early last month feels like it is running on fumes. I have been early in my call that risk asset prices would top, but rising stock prices are about to run into deteriorating economic data, and I don’t think the Federal Reserve will be in a rush to react as soon as the Trump administration was in early April.

I found the chart below telling in terms of how much risk we now have in the market after a historic rise in prices. Taxes on imported goods, otherwise known as tariffs, rose from an effective rate of 2.3% to more than 24% on April 2, sending the major market indexes cascading in the days that followed. That led to a surge in volatility, correlation, and potential for dispersion as measured by option prices. Today, after several delays, rollbacks, and two agreements, the effective rate is approximately 15%. Yet the macro risks reflected in markets are less now than they were before April 2. I think the market is now greatly mispricing risk.

Investors were encouraged by the report that producer prices (PPI) declined last month by 0.5% to an annualized 2.4%, which was below the 2.5% expected and 3.4% we saw in March. The core rate, which excludes food and energy, increased to an annualized 3.1% from 2.5% the month before. As I said with respect to the Consumer Price Index (CPI), I think we are seeing a trough in prices, as tariffs are just starting to have an effect. Inflation is the most lagging of all indicators, which tells us more about where we have been than where we are going.

Retail sales rose just 0.1% in April, but that compares to an upwardly revised 1.7% increase in March, so the annualized rate held steady at a very strong 5.2%. It appears that consumers ramped up purchases of goods in advance of expected price increases, which started to wane some in April, as seven categories out of 13 in this report saw sales declines, led by import-related autos, department stores, apparel, and sporting goods. That freed up some spending for services, as bars and restaurants saw strong sales growth of 1.2%. Again, price increases have not taken a toll yet, but they are coming.

Walmart reported mixed results for the first quarter yesterday, falling a little short on sales with better-than-expected earnings, while same-store sales exceeded estimates with growth of 4.5%. CEO Doug McMillan indicated on the conference call that tariffs were leading to price increases in April and May, and that the “reset of costs” will continue through the year. For the country’s largest retailer, that flies in the face of those who have been advocating that tariffs are not paid by US businesses and consumers. They indisputably are, as evidenced by Walmart.

While I am forecasting an increase in the rate of inflation and deceleration in the rate of consumer spending growth, the proof will show up in the chart below. We are on the warning track when real (inflation-adjusted) personal spending, which includes goods (retail sales) and services, starts to approach zero. In March, the rate was a healthy 3.3%. For me, this is the tip of the spear of an oncoming downturn in the economy. My expectation is that the market will start to discount that downturn, if it happens, well in advance. That’s why I remain cautious over the summer months.

data.title: Meren: A Sustainable 20% FCF Yield And 11% Dividend
data.description:
Africa Oil Corporation (OTCPK:AOIFF) has announced it's changing its name to Meren Energy. The $900 million company recently announced very impressive 1Q 2025 results, as the company has continued to pay-off its balance sheet and drive strong shareholder returns after its merger with Prime Oil and Gas.

Africa Oil Corporation Results

Africa Oil Corporation had more than $50 million in net income from more than 37K barrels in entitlement production.

The company managed to generate a massive $122 million in FCF and YoY it's managed to reduce its net debt position from almost $286 million to almost $192 million. The company's substantial usage in its net debt despite its substantial new dividend of $100 million annualized shows its continued annual cash flow.

Africa Oil Corporation Dividends

The company's new dividend policy is $25 million per quarter or just under $0.04 / share.

The company has announced its latest dividend that will be paid in roughly 4 weeks to shareholders of record at the close of business on May 26. That more than $100 million in annual dividend is a double-digit dividend yield versus the company's present market cap of $900 million, making this one of the few companies with an affordable double-digit dividend.

Africa Oil Corporation Nigeria

The company's core operations are in Nigeria where the entirety of the company's production is located.

This is visible in the company's YoY decline as the company needs to focus on cleaning up its portfolio and generating long-term production. The company is continuing to explore potential assets here for example Akpo Far East that could have 144 million barrels of oil equivalent, which would help the wells here.

The company is continuing to focus on maintaining portfolio production and exploration which is key because it's the company's only producing assets driving all cash flow.

Africa Oil Corporation Namibia

In Namibia, the company is focused on the Venus Field, with a potential FID to be made in H1 2026.

Africa Oil and Gas has a net 4% ownership in the Venus Field through its ownership in Impact Oil & Gas. The new FPSO development plan here is expected to support up to 160K barrels / day of production, which would be more than 6K barrels / day attributable to Africa Oil and Gas. Respectable but not enough to replace its core production.

Still this is a key development for the late 2020s and additional drilling campaigns here are incredibly important. This is a multi-billion barrel field that could support hundreds of Ks of barrels / day in production for the company.

Africa Oil Corporation Guidance

The company's guidance for 2025 shows production coming in below its 1Q 2025 results as uncertainty remains.

The company is basing things on $75 / barrel Brent implying a 12% reduction versus the midpoint on the financial guidance. That would imply roughly $310 million of cash flow at the midpoint, which after $170 million in capital investment, would imply $140 million in FCF for the company. That's enough for the company to comfortably cover its dividend.

The company paying down its debt is key to reducing roughly $60 million in annual interest expenses which would be key to the company improving its FCF and shareholder returns. It would more than double the company's post dividend FCF.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is crude oil prices. The company is very profitable at current levels and it's continuing to improve its debt load and drive shareholder returns. We'd like to see more share buybacks at current prices, however, at $65 / Brent, with the potential to fall further, that'd hurt the company's FCF substantially.

Conclusion

Africa Oil Corporation is a great company with an impressive portfolio of assets. The company is worth almost $1 billion today and it's paying out a double-digit dividend yield. At the same time, the company is using its additional cash flow to rapidly improving its cash position and pay down its debt.

Given its higher bond cost we'd like to see it use cash to pay-down debt further helping reduce its $60 million in annual interest expenditures. The company partially did that with a further $80 million RBL balance reduction post quarter end. We have some concerns about the company's future long-term production, but with exciting exploration assets, we're confident in its ability to drive long-term shareholder returns.

data.title: Main Street Capital: Why The Market Keeps Paying A Premium
data.description:
Introduction

Main Street Capital (NYSE:MAIN) is not the most famous dividend stock on the market, but it might be one of the most stable and effective. In an industry often defined by yield-chasing and aggressive leverage, MAIN is focused on patience, discipline, and long-term strategy. The way MAIN continues to build value, quarter after quarter, without overpromising or overextending, is what makes this company truly special.

Company Overview

The company has been operating as a business development company [BDC] for almost two decades. They use 3 main strategies: providing debt and equity to lower middle market companies, investing in private loans, and managing external assets through its advisory business. The lower middle market segment is their main segment. This is money lending and investing in privately owned new, growing companies, which are based in the U.S. These are not large and mature corporations, but rather growing businesses where Main Street can add value. And sometimes, that value compounds beautifully.

That’s exactly what we saw this quarter with the exit of Heritage Vet Partners. Quick explanation - MAIN doesn’t just lend money to companies and collect interest like most BDCs. In some cases, especially with smaller, growing private businesses, they also take a small ownership stake in the company. This is called equity investment. In this case, MAIN invested in a company called Heritage Vet Partners back in 2020. They put in $7 million of equity, and now they just sold it and made $55.5 million in profit. This deal generated an internal rate of return [IRR] of 72% and a 10x multiple on invested capital. This exit was basically a culmination of a strategy that MAIN has been applying for years. This is the main difference of MAIN, compared to its peers. They are not just lending money, and I think this strategy is simply great.

Q1 2025

MAIN just reported another strong quarter. Despite a minor earnings miss on an adjusted basis, just $0.01 below consensus, Main Street’s overall results were anything but weak.

GAAP earnings beat the expectations by $0.27, and the company’s DNII of $1.07 per share easily covered both the regular and supplemental dividends. The slight miss in adjusted earnings was due to a drop in fee income and lower floating-rate interest income, not to any deterioration in portfolio quality.

Net asset value per share (NAV) continues to grow, and it has just hit a record of $32.03. Including this quarter, MAIN's NAV has grown for the past 11 consecutive quarters, which is incredible. Return on equity was 16.5%, which would be very impressive in any industry, but it's especially a great result for the BDC, because they must give away most of their earnings as dividends, and they can reinvest only a very small part. 16.5% reflects MAIN’s ability to create real value from its portfolio by growing NAV and exiting equity positions in time for the maximum profit.

Another important factor is how management continues to handle shareholder returns. MAIN declared a $0.30 per share supplemental dividend for June (15th in a row). Regular monthly dividends have also been raised for Q3, from $0.23 to $0.255 per share. This is not a big deal, of course, but in uncertain times, when the rest of the BDCs are being cautious (some are cutting the dividend), the fact that MAIN raises the regular payment suggests that the management is more than confident about the future. By the way, on the graph below, you can see regular to supplemental dividends since 2007.

Why Consider MAIN?

First, the company is internally managed. It means that, unlike most BDCs, MAIN doesn’t pay huge management fees to an external advisor. As a result, more of the income stays in the company, and instead of third-party managers, all of the extra profit goes to shareholders, which is great. Because of this, by the way, their expense ratio is one of the lowest in the industry. Besides, this structure makes sure that the interests of management are the same as investors, which is rare, especially in the financial sector.

Another metric that deserves special mention it's MAIN’s dividends. Unlike many of its peers, the company is not trying to attract as many investors as possible by putting the highest yields or by paying out everything they've got. Their strategy is completely different. The company tries to carefully predict what the income will be and to set the dividend accordingly, or maybe even a little lower, just in case the predictions turn out to be wrong. This is why their payout is one of the most sustainable, and this is the reason for the frequent supplemental dividend.

Supplemental dividends are extra payments, like the $0.30 one declared this quarter. These are announced only when the company has earned more than it needs to cover its base dividend. That way, investors get a bonus they did not expect to get, because the company did not promise anything. This is genius, because first of all, people always react to the surprises better than to the pre-planned news, results, or in this case, dividend. Besides, this is the reason why in the most difficult times, like in 2020 and 2022-2023, MAIN did not cut the dividend. They simply didn't add the supplement, and no one was upset about this.

So, MAIN does not pay out everything it gets. The company keeps some cash in-house (as much as they can), and this is the reason for the steadily growing NAV. The steadily growing NAV shows investors that the business becomes more valuable, and as a result, the stock trades at a premium. If you check the valuation of this company over the years, you will see that being overvalued it's a norm for MAIN.

Risks and Considerations

Of course, no investment is without risk. MAIN is a part of these unstable economic conditions. In Q1, total investment income decreased slightly from the quarter before that, because of the lower interest and fee income. Part of this was due to a small increase in non-accrual investments and a reduction in benchmark interest rates, which hurt floating rate income. There’s also macro uncertainty to consider. Tariffs, for example, are still concerning, as they should be, because the situation can change at any moment. Tariff pause can get undone. MAIN’s management noted that the majority of its portfolio companies are based in the U.S. and have limited direct exposure, but at the same time, they also pointed out that around 10–20% of holdings could be affected anyway. The team is actively working with portfolio companies to mitigate these risks, but what their success rate is, we will know only in the future.

Investing strategy

It's easy to come to the idea that to invest in MAIN, you should wait until the price drops. At least, mathematically speaking, this sounds like the most logical thing to do. With the current annualized dividend payout around $3.06 per share, buying at $53 gets you a yield of about 5.8%. But if the price drops to $50, the yield could rise to 6.1%. And at $45, it would be 6.8%. So yeah, lower price = bigger yield. But the question is, should you wait and miss the possible monthly income you could get if you invested early?

I am not so sure. MAIN is one of the few BDCs that consistently trade at a premium to NAV, as I explained previously. Investors see the consistency, the risk management, and the perfect track record, so when dips happen, they’re usually not dramatic and very short, so there is no guarantee you will catch them. Besides, trying to time your entry often means missing out on profit in the meantime. MAIN is as safe as BDCs can get, so I would be slowly adding to this position over time.

Final Thoughts

If you are looking for quick returns, MAIN is not for you. There is no much excitement about this company. But for those building long-term income portfolios, MAIN is a great, although conservative, candidate. There are bigger BDCs, of course, and there are definitely those with much higher dividend yields, and I am not saying Main is the best choice. It’s definitely one of the safest choices, and in the current market, it’s the most important metric for me. After the tariff pause, the optimism seems to be coming back slowly, but the recession is still possible, and the market is still too volatile to trust this “happiness”. The rating is a Buy.

data.title: Foot Locker Deal Calls For Some Fancy Footwork
data.description:
By Breakingviews

Dick’s Sporting Goods (DKS) is taking a dangerous step. The U.S. retailer of everything from golf clubs to soccer balls agreed to buy sneaker chain Foot Locker (FL) for $2.4 billion, expanding its presence internationally and giving it greater negotiating power with vendors. At the price it’s paying, however, boss Lauren Hobart cannot afford any stumbles.

This would be the biggest acquisition in Dick's 77-year history, adding a network of 2,400 stores across 26 countries. For the privilege, the buyer is offering Foot Locker’s investors $24 a share in cash, 87% higher than where it was trading pre-deal, or stock if they prefer. The premium adds up to almost $1.1 billion.

To justify the rationale, Dick’s anticipates between $100 million and $125 million of cost savings from the deal. Taxed at the standard U.S. corporate rate and capitalized, these synergies are worth nearly $900 million today, according to Breakingviews calculations. The 20% shortfall against the premium helps explain why Dick’s shares tumbled 14%, erasing the entire value of the deal.

There may be more than meets the eye, however. The combined company, if trustbusters sign off on the transaction, would control more than a fifth of the $41 billion U.S. athletic footwear market, according to UBS analysts. Owning Foot Locker also would nearly double the proportion of revenue that Dick’s generates from sneakers to roughly 50%.

As a result, it may be able to squeeze suppliers like Nike, which Dick’s will be increasingly dependent upon, even more than it is flagging. History suggests that retailers can slash costs equal to as much as 3% of a takeover target’s top line, which would imply a figure nearly twice as high as the top end of the touted range.

About 100 U.S. Foot Locker locations also overlap with Dick’s, Morgan Stanley analysts reckon, implying lease savings from less profitable shops. Cheaper sneakers also might attract more customers into stores, where they’re apt to pick up higher-margin apparel, too, and there’s scope to beef up online sales.

There’s little room for error, however, which will be harder to avoid as U.S. tariffs increase. The implied return on invested capital, using Foot Locker’s $205 million of expected 2026 operating income, based on LSEG data, and $125 million of savings, would exceed 10%. Dick’s weighted average cost of capital is about the same, Morningstar calculates. To make the deal a success will require some fancy financial footwork.

Context News

Dick’s Sporting Goods said on May 15 that it has agreed to buy rival Foot Locker for $2.4 billion, with ambitions to grow the sneaker chain by applying its operational knowhow. The combined company would have an estimated $21 billion of revenue from nearly 3,300 stores. Under the terms of the deal, Dick’s agreed to pay $24 a share in cash or 0.1168 of its shares for each Foot Locker share. The buyer anticipates generating between $100 million and $125 million of cost savings in the “medium term.” Goldman Sachs is advising Dick’s and Evercore is advising Foot Locker.

Original Post
